speaker: interviewer
text: Today is November 10, 1995. My name is Amy Godin. I'm conducting an interview with Lisa Murik in Silver Spring, Maryland. And the language is English. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Today is November 10, 1995. My name is Amy Godin. G-O-D-I-N. I'm conducting an interview with Lisa Murik in Silver Spring, Maryland, USA. The language is English. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What is your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lisa Murik.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-I-S-A M-U-R-I-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lisa Dawidowicz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, boy. L-I-S-A D-A-W-I-D-O-W-I-C-Z.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go by any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: November 5, 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age?

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you spell the city where you were born?

speaker: interviewee
text: O-S-T-R-O-G.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you pronounce that?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ostrog.

speaker: interviewer
text: Okay. And what country was that in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Poland. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Okay. Could you tell me what your family was like before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were a very close family. And we were observant. My mother had a business, my mother and father. And I went to school. And everything was like a normal family would be.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any brothers or sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had two sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Edith and Anne.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What was your daily life like in Ostrog?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I got up in the morning, I went to school, came home, and did homework, and then went out to play. And that's how it went on the whole time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were the names of your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Elias. And my mother's name is [? Gossietoba. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you very close to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very close to my father and mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the Jewish community like in Ostrog?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was-- was 1,000 people in our community, in our city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a religious community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was half-and-half like.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe the Jewish life in Ostrog before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, everybody was observant. Came a holiday, the stores were closed. Everybody went to the synagogue. People got together on the holidays. Like, you know, like here, the same. Not the same, because here some businesses are open. In our city everything was closed down.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that your mother had a business.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother and father together.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was their business?

speaker: interviewee
text: They had a grocery, wholesale/retail business until the war broke out. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Okay. You mentioned in the pre-interview that you left school at grade seven.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, the Russians came in first in our city. And I went to Russian school too for a while. And then the Germans came in chased the Russians out, and they took it over.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, what language did you speak growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish and Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Okay. Did your family belong to Jewish organizations before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, No.

speaker: interviewer
text: They belonged to a synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the name of the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's hard to remember. I wish I had that picture. I gave it to the museum.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the relationship between the Jews and the non-Jews in Ostrog before the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, everybody was gettin' along. There was no problems. In fact, my mother had business. She had, you know, different people come and shop, non-Jews and Jews. And everything was normal till the war broke out, really till the Germans came.

speaker: interviewer
text: What types of literature would be found in your home when you were young?

speaker: interviewee
text: All kind of books. It's hard to remember now. It was all kind of books in the house-- prayer books, non-prayer books, reading books.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any anti-Semitism in Ostrog before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not- not-- Before the war was not that bad. Now, during the war then there was more anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Did you know about Hitler's rise to power in the '30s?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We-- you see, they came-- the German came first to Warsaw, you know, that's part of Poland. And my grandfather, Josef, he died when the Germans came into their hometown. And they wrote us a postcard that he died with natural causes. So we could not understand what that meant, that he was not killed by the Germans that meant. Later we find that out, when we got-- you know, the Germans came to us and then started the killings. So we then understood what that postcard meant.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that?

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland. They lived in Lodz, near Lodz, my grandparents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to your life before the war. Describe the community. What was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: What what do you mean by that? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there a-- a lot of Jews? Were there a lot of shops?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there were a lot of Jewish shops. There was a lot of Jewish population, and everybody was close. You know, people were close, very-- like one family. It was very nice, as much as I can remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. When did the Germans first occupy Ostrog?

speaker: interviewee
text: Let's see, they came, it was the fall, I think, either '41 or '42. I don't remember exactly. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And-- well, they were fighting with the Russians before they occupied our city. This I remember very clearly.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was-- the day was a Thursday. This I remember, because my mother was baking bread. And my father worked for the Russians, and he came home from the office. And he said that looks like there'll be a war between the Russians and Germans. And the Russians, his office offered give wagon horses for people that like to leave the town and go to Russia. And my mother said, whatever had to happen will happen right here. I'm not running no place. And that same day, they started bombing and, you know, everything. And we had to run and to hide. And then, that's when the Russians left us, and the Germans came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember what year that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like I said, either '41, '40 or '41. I'm not exactly. Or '42 maybe. It's hard to remember the year. And at beginning, when they came, the Germans, we lived in our own houses. And that was already in the summer. The first-- the first killing of the Jews was summer, the month, I think, of July. And at that time, nobody knew what they would do. One morning they knocked on everybody's doors early in the morning and started chasing everybody out the houses. And who didn't leave they shooted right there on place.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said that was spring what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that was summer.

speaker: interviewer
text: Summer.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they got us all together and they started marching us. Our city was divided by a river. Was like two parts of the city. So they started marching us. And right next to us, the Ukrainian people were walking with the shovels. Now, we didn't understood at that time what they, you know, were doing. And then they got us across the bridge to a big field. And at there, they were already standing with the machine guns, the German soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we got in there. And they only took us like around a-- inside the circle. It was-- I remember, it was a very hot day. And we didn't know what they could do. We didn't know they were gonna kill us or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then they started separating the people. And they took all the men, young and old, and marched them away. Nobody knew where they took 'em, or what they would do. Was your father part of that group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Part too, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, maybe 16 or 15. 15, 16, something like that. And then one of the German soldiers walked over to one of the people and said, women and children will leave. So at that time, everybody who had a few kids started-- you know, even other people who didn't have children. And then they got all the women with children separate, And they started marching us over the bridge. And soon we got over the bridge, they told us to run. So everybody ran wherever they could. And right after that, we heard shooting. By that time, a little was understood what they meant to do with everybody else. Now, we still don't know where the men were. It was already late evening, the men came back. They took them toward there-- there was like an armory here. They took them to work on the armory, get ready for the soldiers come to the city. That was the first time when they did something like that. And they organized a Judenrat. It was an office where the Germans came and asked people to come to work that was run by Jewish people. And I-- I went to work almost every day, different places.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now what year are we talking about now?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the first year when they came into us.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so like, 1941? 42?

speaker: interviewee
text: '41 or '42, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was the ghetto established in Ostrog?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ghetto was established-- I don't remember the year. It was quite a few months before they established the ghetto. They made a high fence around one section of the city. And in one house could live two, three families.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the first situation that you described when you walked over the bridge--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, That was before the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was before. And then you went home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and then I started working every day. They took us different places to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father come home that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that night, my father came home. Most of the men came home. And everybody was organized to go to work. You had to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked on railroad stations and at schools we-- once we went to a school to clean up, get ready for the soldiers. And that time, I-- I really used my head. What I mean by that, they took a few men. Was wintertime. They took a few men to chop wood to make fire. In Europe, they didn't have natural gas like here. You know, when you have to heat it up, a house, they have a special like a brick oven. And you put wood in it, and that's what heated up the house.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they took a few men and a few girls-- I was one of them-- to a high school to get ready for the Army come in. And we had to put the straw down on the floors, get everything clean. And the men had to chop up the wood outside. It was freezing weather. Now, the Germans didn't care.

speaker: interviewee
text: At that time, we only had one German watching us. So after we finished, we were ready to go back. And he told us we couldn't leave. We had to wait for the soldiers to come.

speaker: interviewee
text: So somehow, it hit me it might not work out great. And he didn't let us go. So I ran outside and I told the man to try to keep him busy outside so we can run away. So he-- one of the men called the German out. He talked to him. We ran into the office, to the Judenrat. The man at the Judenrat put us in a second room. He put a cabinet in front of the door because he was afraid the German might come. And sure enough, he came and asked for us, for the young girls. And he told him, the building is big. We might be hiding someplace. And he left.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when it got dark, we went home. And that-- life went on like this for a long time. And then once they took us to work on a railroad station. And they had a barn where we stayed. And we were a big group of women and men in that barn. We were supposed to work a whole week. And when it came by the end of the week, like a Thursday, one of the women who made that water soup for us in the kitchen, when we were eating, she said to me quietly that the next day after we finished working-- that's gonna be Friday-- they gonna blow up the building with us together. If anybody wants to run or do something about it, to let her know, she'll help.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that night when we got back to the barn to sleep, I got everybody together. And I sat and I told them what the woman said. They said, no. They don't believe that it's gonna happen, and everybody will go home, you know, and all that. So I told them, I'm leaving. If I have to die, doesn't matter if I die in the barn or if I die on the road. I just gotta take that chance. So next morning when we went for the cup of water with the bread. So I told the woman I'm ready to go. So she had a long skirt from you-- that shawl like those, you know, farmers would wear there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I stayed behind. And I got dressed. And they took me on the horse and wagon just out of the-- that little station. And then I had to walk maybe about 40, 50 kilometer home to the ghetto. And by the time I got home, it was already nighttime. And I knocked on the door. And my mother was surprised to see me. She said, I didn't expect you to come home tonight. Tomorrow. I says, let me in. I'm tired and I'll tell you all what happened. The next day, another person ran away by himself, and he told me I was right. They blew up the whole building with everybody in it. That was the second time they did that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then one day they came to the ghetto with a truck. And they never came to the ghetto with a truck. Because when you go to work, you had to go to that office. So when I saw it, I said to my mother, no. I'm not going to work today. She says, why? I says, they never come with a truck, and there's something suspicious. So in front of the-- where we lived was a store boarded up because it was broken, you know, and all that. So I climbed in there. And I watched through the cracks like they were loading up all young people, not old people at all, all young people. And they were putting them in the truck like one on top of another. So I said, that's it. Something is gonna happen. And sure enough, they never came back. And they were shot in the woods, we find it out later.

speaker: interviewee
text: And life went on like this. And I been working all the time till 1943. 1943 was, I think, it was-- it would be September, when they came the last time with a-- a lot of trucks to the ghetto to get everybody else away already.

speaker: interviewee
text: So by that time, everybody got smart already and made themselves some hideouts in the house. But where we lived, we made a hideout under the steps going to the basement. So when we woke up that morning, and we saw all those trucks, we were-- you know, they could figure-- my father and mother figured out that something be not right, and we climbed into that hideout. Now, we didn't have any food. We didn't have nothing in there because we never knew when they will come. So you couldn't get ready for it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And sure enough, they started chasing everybody. We could hear the soldiers walking in the house, looking probably for people, and chasing everybody out. And then it got quiet. And it looks like everybody was gone. And we sat there for three days. On the third day-- there was a main water pipe in there. The water pipe busted. And we had no choice but leave, because the water started coming up-- up to our necks. And so we waited till the evening. And we came out of there. We went into the house. In the dark, we changed our clothes, because we couldn't make anything, you know, lights, because you didn't know who's walking around outside. And my mother had prepared us a place we had to hide. Across from the ghetto, there lived Christian people. And one of those people, my mother, you know, gave them money, gave them whatever we had so they will hide us out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when-- after we changed clothes, there was another house in front of ours. So we went over to the other house. And then we looked at the road, and we didn't see nobody walking. Ran across the street to this man's house.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother knocked on the door. And the man said, if you're not gonna leave, I'm calling the Germans. And I don't care what happens to me. He said, but you will be killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we saw we-- it's not good. So we went to our next door neighbor, which my mother knew also, those people. And the man said that he could keep us overnight. Because he lives right by the road and he has no place. He had a stack of hay sitting on the-- outside. So he said we could climb in there and take a chance till the next night. So we climbed over in there, and he brought us some food. And he brought us some clothes what looked like Ukrainian people, not, you know, Jewish people. And the next day, in the evening, we climbed out of there, and we started walking. And my mother was the hero. She was in charge. And she kept just tellin' us that God be with us, and we'll be saved. Were your-- was your family with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the whole family-- my father and my two sisters. So we walked the outskirts of the city. And my mother-- we went into a Polish woman because there were, you know, Ukrainian and Polish people, they were in the town. And when we came in her yard, we heard Germans speaking in the house. So she had like a little shack where she used to keep a pig in it. So we climbed into the shack and to wait till the Germans leave. After the Germans left, my mother knocked on the door. And the woman said she would kept us. Now, her daughters are dating Germans. And she's afraid the daughters will see us and, you know, tell the Germans about us. Now, we can stay overnight again.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we stayed overnight there. And next morning, real early morning before the sun came out, my mother left, and left me, my two sisters, and my father behind, and she went to look for a place for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was gone all day. And we were really worried about her, because we didn't know what, you know, happened. So then the evening she came back. And she says, children, I got a place, and I hope to be safe-- we'll be safe.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the next morning we left there. We went to this woman's house. My mother didn't know-- nobody-- we didn't know her at all. She was a stranger. Now, when we got there, she says, she will keep us as long she can. And because God send us there. She-- she believed in God. She was our-- she couldn't read. She couldn't write.

speaker: interviewee
text: And first, she put us in a barn, and then she realized that that place wouldn't be safe because she was only one house there, and around her was a lot of field, and people came to work in these fields.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she said-- in Europe they used to have a big hole in the ground. And they kept potatoes in there for the winter. So she went and took the potatoes out. And she put straw down. And she put a big pole in the middle and put boards on top.

speaker: interviewee
text: And was the fall when we got to her. So she put a lot of pumpkins on top. And she left one little opening so we could go down there. And we all climbed down there, and we stayed in there for 16 months till we got liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's go back and talk about the ghetto some more. You mentioned the name of the ghetto was Ostrog.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, do you know how-- what the size of the ghetto was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't tell.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember some of the names of the streets?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You mentioned that there was, in the beginning, a fence, I believe, set up between the area when they were setting up the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe what it was like in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, everybody was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] you want me to keep going?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the life was very strange because everybody was afraid. They didn't know what the next day will bring because when you went to work, the SS was there with the-- with the whips, standing right over you, and you were very scared. And you have to stay in the houses. When you get back, you couldn't go no place. You have to stay in your own place. You were like in a jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
